question: Felicia wants to deploy an encryption solution that will protect files in motion as they are copied between file shares as well as at rest, and also needs it to support granular, per-user security. What type of solution should she select?
explanation: File encryption protects individual files at rest and can continue protecting them when they are copied, while also supporting granular per-user access.
simpleExplanation: The question is asking you to recognise the Security+ concept that best fits the situation. The key clue points to File encryption. In simple words, File encryption protects individual files at rest and can continue protecting them when they are copied, while also supporting granular per-user access.
whyCorrect: File encryption protects individual files at rest and can continue protecting them when they are copied, while also supporting granular per-user access.

whyIncorrect.A: Partition encryption protects a specific disk partition. It does not normally follow individual files between shares or provide granular per-user file permissions. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to File encryption. Partition/full-disk encryption protect larger storage areas and do not give per-user file-level control; record-level encryption is mainly for database records, not ordinary files moving between shares.
whyIncorrect.C: Full-disk encryption protects an entire disk when the device is off or locked. It is mainly device-loss protection, not granular per-file control. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to File encryption. Partition/full-disk encryption protect larger storage areas and do not give per-user file-level control; record-level encryption is mainly for database records, not ordinary files moving between shares.
whyIncorrect.D: Record-level encryption protects individual database records. It is normally a database concept, not ordinary file-share protection. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to File encryption. Partition/full-disk encryption protect larger storage areas and do not give per-user file-level control; record-level encryption is mainly for database records, not ordinary files moving between shares.

fullExplanation:
The correct answer is B. File encryption.

In Simple Words: What is this asking?
The question is asking you to recognise the Security+ concept that best fits the situation. The key clue points to File encryption. In simple words, File encryption protects individual files at rest and can continue protecting them when they are copied, while also supporting granular per-user access.

Why this answer is correct:
File encryption protects individual files at rest and can continue protecting them when they are copied, while also supporting granular per-user access.

Why the other options are incorrect:
A. Partition encryption: Partition encryption protects a specific disk partition. It does not normally follow individual files between shares or provide granular per-user file permissions. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to File encryption. Partition/full-disk encryption protect larger storage areas and do not give per-user file-level control; record-level encryption is mainly for database records, not ordinary files moving between shares.
C. Full-disk encryption: Full-disk encryption protects an entire disk when the device is off or locked. It is mainly device-loss protection, not granular per-file control. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to File encryption. Partition/full-disk encryption protect larger storage areas and do not give per-user file-level control; record-level encryption is mainly for database records, not ordinary files moving between shares.
D. Record-level encryption: Record-level encryption protects individual database records. It is normally a database concept, not ordinary file-share protection. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to File encryption. Partition/full-disk encryption protect larger storage areas and do not give per-user file-level control; record-level encryption is mainly for database records, not ordinary files moving between shares.

question: Valerie wants to use a certificate to handle multiple subdomains for her website, including the sales.example.com and support.example.com subdomains. What type of certificate should she use?
explanation: A wildcard certificate such as *.example.com can cover multiple subdomains under the same parent domain.
simpleExplanation: The question is asking you to recognise the Security+ concept that best fits the situation. The key clue points to A wildcard certificate. In simple words, A wildcard certificate such as *.example.com can cover multiple subdomains under the same parent domain.
whyCorrect: A wildcard certificate such as *.example.com can cover multiple subdomains under the same parent domain.

whyIncorrect.A: A self-signed certificate is signed by its own owner rather than by a trusted certificate authority, so clients may not trust it automatically. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to A wildcard certificate. Self-signed certificates are not trusted by public browsers, a root-of-trust certificate is not for covering subdomains, and a CRL is for revocation, not identity coverage.
whyIncorrect.B: A root of trust is the trusted starting point for a chain of trust, not a certificate type used to cover many web subdomains. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to A wildcard certificate. Self-signed certificates are not trusted by public browsers, a root-of-trust certificate is not for covering subdomains, and a CRL is for revocation, not identity coverage.
whyIncorrect.C: A certificate revocation list is used to list certificates that should no longer be trusted. It is not used to cover subdomains. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to A wildcard certificate. Self-signed certificates are not trusted by public browsers, a root-of-trust certificate is not for covering subdomains, and a CRL is for revocation, not identity coverage.

fullExplanation:
The correct answer is D. A wildcard certificate.

Why the other options are incorrect:

It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to A wildcard certificate. Self-signed certificates are not trusted by public browsers, a root-of-trust certificate is not for covering subdomains, and a CRL is for revocation, not identity coverage.

options.C: Compensating controls and the controls they are replacing

explanation: Gap analysis compares the control objectives with the controls currently intended to meet those objectives.
answerText: Control objectives and controls intended to meet the objectives
simpleExplanation: The question is asking you to recognise the Security+ concept that best fits the situation. The key clue points to Control objectives and controls intended to meet the objectives. question: Using a tool like git is most frequently associated with what critical change management process?
explanation: Git is a version control tool because it tracks file versions, changes, and history.
simpleExplanation: Git is the clue. Git records versions of files, shows what changed, allows rollback to earlier versions, and tracks who changed what. In change management, that is version control.
whyCorrect: Git is a version control tool because it tracks file versions, changes, and history.

whyIncorrect.A: A backout plan, also called a rollback plan, is the pre-written plan for undoing a change if it fails. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to Version control. A backout plan is rollback, stakeholder analysis identifies affected parties, and SOPs document procedures; Git's main purpose is tracking versions and changes.
whyIncorrect.B: This option describes Stakeholder analysis. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to Version control. A backout plan is rollback, stakeholder analysis identifies affected parties, and SOPs document procedures; Git's main purpose is tracking versions and changes.
whyIncorrect.D: Standard operating procedures are documented, repeatable instructions for carrying out routine processes consistently. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to Version control. A backout plan is rollback, stakeholder analysis identifies affected parties, and SOPs document procedures; Git's main purpose is tracking versions and changes.

fullExplanation:
The correct answer is C. Version control.

Git is the clue. Git records versions of files, shows what changed, allows rollback to earlier versions, and tracks who changed what. In change management, that is version control.

Why the other options are incorrect:
A. Having a backout plan: A backout plan, also called a rollback plan, is the pre-written plan for undoing a change if it fails. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to Version control. A backout plan is rollback, stakeholder analysis identifies affected parties, and SOPs document procedures; Git's main purpose is tracking versions and changes.
B. Stakeholder analysis: This option describes Stakeholder analysis. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to Version control. A backout plan is rollback, stakeholder analysis identifies affected parties, and SOPs document procedures; Git's main purpose is tracking versions and changes.
D. Standard operating procedures (SOPs): Standard operating procedures are documented, repeatable instructions for carrying out routine processes consistently. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to Version control. A backout plan is rollback, stakeholder analysis identifies affected parties, and SOPs document procedures; Git's main purpose is tracking versions and changes.

question: Jacob is concerned that the password used for one of his organization's services is weak, and he wants to make it harder to crack by making it harder to test possible keys during a brute-force attack. What is this technique called?
explanation: Key stretching makes brute-force attempts slower by increasing the work needed to test each possible password or key.
simpleExplanation: The question is asking you to recognise the Security+ concept that best fits the situation. The key clue points to Key stretching. In simple words, Key stretching makes brute-force attempts slower by increasing the work needed to test each possible password or key.
whyCorrect: Key stretching makes brute-force attempts slower by increasing the work needed to test each possible password or key.

whyIncorrect.A: Master keying is a key hierarchy or shared access arrangement. It is not a password-hardening technique. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to Key stretching. Master keying and key rotation are key-management ideas, and passphrase armoring is not the standard term; key stretching deliberately slows brute-force testing.
whyIncorrect.C: Key rotation means replacing keys on a schedule or after risk, not making each brute-force guess harder. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to Key stretching. Master keying and key rotation are key-management ideas, and passphrase armoring is not the standard term; key stretching deliberately slows brute-force testing.
whyIncorrect.D: This option describes Passphrase armoring. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to Key stretching. The correct answer is B. Key stretching.

question: Rick wants to make offline brute-force attacks against his password file very difficult for attackers. Which of the following is not a common technique to make passwords harder to crack?

options.C: Use of a purpose-built password hashing algorithm
options.D: Encrypting password plain text using symmetric encryption

explanation: Passwords should normally be hashed with salts and strong password-hashing methods, not stored as encrypted plaintext.
answerText: Encrypting password plain text using symmetric encryption
simpleExplanation: The question is asking you to recognise the Security+ concept that best fits the situation. The key clue points to Encrypting password plain text using symmetric encryption. In simple words, Passwords should normally be hashed with salts and strong password-hashing methods, not stored as encrypted plaintext.
whyCorrect: Passwords should normally be hashed with salts and strong password-hashing methods, not stored as encrypted plaintext.

whyIncorrect.A: A salt is a unique value added to a password before hashing so identical passwords do not produce identical hashes and rainbow tables are less useful. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to Encrypting password plain text using symmetric encryption. Salt, pepper, and password-hashing algorithms are standard password-hardening methods; storing passwords by reversible symmetric encryption is not the normal safe method.
whyIncorrect.B: A pepper is a secret value added to password hashing, usually stored separately from the password database. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to Encrypting password plain text using symmetric encryption. Salt, pepper, and password-hashing algorithms are standard password-hardening methods; storing passwords by reversible symmetric encryption is not the normal safe method.
whyIncorrect.C: A purpose-built password hashing algorithm is designed to make password cracking slower and harder. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to Encrypting password plain text using symmetric encryption. Salt, pepper, and password-hashing algorithms are standard password-hardening methods; storing passwords by reversible symmetric encryption is not the normal safe method.

question: Sally wants to ensure that her change management process includes a procedure for what to do if the change fails. What should she create to handle this possibility?
explanation: A backout plan is the pre-written recovery or rollback plan used if a change fails.
simpleExplanation: The question is asking: "What do you call an IT escape plan or emergency exit strategy?" Imagine an IT team is upgrading a major system over the weekend. If they hit a serious error halfway through the upgrade and the service breaks, they cannot leave the system broken for Monday morning. They need a pre-written, step-by-step manual for undoing the change and safely returning the system to the previous working state. In change management, that safety net is called a backout plan, sometimes called a rollback plan.
whyCorrect: A backout plan is correct because it explains exactly what the team should do if the change fails. It is prepared before the work starts so the team is not guessing during an outage.

whyIncorrect.A: An impact analysis is done before the change to understand who or what might be affected. It helps predict risk, but it is not the procedure for undoing a failed change.
whyIncorrect.C: A regression test checks whether old features still work after a change. Testing may reveal a failure, but the test itself is not the recovery plan.
whyIncorrect.D: A maintenance window is the approved time slot for doing the work, such as Saturday 2:00 AM to 4:00 AM. It is a time period, not the emergency rollback procedure.

fullExplanation:
The correct answer is B. A backout plan.

In Simple Words: What is this asking?
The question is asking: "What do you call an IT escape plan or emergency exit strategy?" Imagine an IT team is upgrading a major system over the weekend. If they hit a serious error halfway through the upgrade and the service breaks, they cannot leave the system broken for Monday morning. They need a pre-written, step-by-step manual for undoing the change and safely returning the system to the previous working state. In change management, that safety net is called a backout plan, sometimes called a rollback plan.

Why this answer is correct:
A backout plan is correct because it explains exactly what the team should do if the change fails. It is prepared before the work starts so the team is not guessing during an outage.

Why the other options are incorrect:
A. An impact analysis: An impact analysis is done before the change to understand who or what might be affected. It helps predict risk, but it is not the procedure for undoing a failed change.
C. A regression test: A regression test checks whether old features still work after a change. Testing may reveal a failure, but the test itself is not the recovery plan.
D. A maintenance window: A maintenance window is the approved time slot for doing the work, such as Saturday 2:00 AM to 4:00 AM. It is a time period, not the emergency rollback procedure.

question: Charles wants to reduce the threat scope of compromised credentials. What type of the following security controls is best suited to meeting this need?
explanation: Zero trust reduces the scope of damage from compromised credentials by limiting implicit trust and continuously evaluating access.
simpleExplanation: The question is asking you to recognise the Security+ concept that best fits the situation. The key clue points to Zero trust. In simple words, Zero trust reduces the scope of damage from compromised credentials by limiting implicit trust and continuously evaluating access.
whyCorrect: Zero trust reduces the scope of damage from compromised credentials by limiting implicit trust and continuously evaluating access.

whyIncorrect.A: Single sign-on lets users authenticate once and access multiple services, but it does not by itself reduce the damage from a stolen password as strongly as continuous zero-trust decisions. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to Zero trust. SSO can centralise login, federation shares identity, and MFA helps credential security, but zero trust is the model designed to limit implicit trust and reduce blast radius after credential compromise.
whyIncorrect.B: Federation lets organizations or systems trust identities from another identity provider. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to Zero trust. SSO can centralise login, federation shares identity, and MFA helps credential security, but zero trust is the model designed to limit implicit trust and reduce blast radius after credential compromise.
whyIncorrect.D: Multifactor authentication requires more than one factor, such as something you know plus something you have. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to Zero trust. SSO can centralise login, federation shares identity, and MFA helps credential security, but zero trust is the model designed to limit implicit trust and reduce blast radius after credential compromise.

fullExplanation:
The correct answer is C. Zero trust.

question: Selah's organization has recently experienced a breach and the private keys for her organization's certificates were exposed. What should she immediately do?

options.A: Reissue the certificates with changed hostnames and other details.
options.B: Replace the certificates with self-signed certificates until they can be replaced by the vendor.
options.C: Revoke the certificates and place them on a certificate revocation list.
options.D: Replace the certificates with wildcard certificates.

explanation: Exposed certificate private keys require revocation so systems stop trusting the affected certificates.
answerText: Revoke the certificates and place them on a certificate revocation list.
simpleExplanation: The question is asking you to recognise the Security+ concept that best fits the situation. In simple words, Exposed certificate private keys require revocation so systems stop trusting the affected certificates.
whyCorrect: Exposed certificate private keys require revocation so systems stop trusting the affected certificates.

whyIncorrect.A: Certificates can identify users, systems, or services by binding an identity to a public key. Changing hostnames, using self-signed certificates, or switching to wildcard certificates does not remove trust in the compromised certificates; revocation does.
whyIncorrect.B: A self-signed certificate is signed by its own owner rather than by a trusted certificate authority, so clients may not trust it automatically. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to Revoke the certificates and place them on a certificate revocation list.. Changing hostnames, using self-signed certificates, or switching to wildcard certificates does not remove trust in the compromised certificates; revocation does.
whyIncorrect.D: A wildcard certificate uses a pattern such as *.example.com so one certificate can cover multiple subdomains under the same parent domain. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to Revoke the certificates and place them on a certificate revocation list.. Changing hostnames, using self-signed certificates, or switching to wildcard certificates does not remove trust in the compromised certificates; revocation does.

question: When is data on a drive that uses full-disk encryption at the greatest risk?

explanation: Full-disk encryption offers least protection after the user logs in and the disk is unlocked for use.
answerText: When the system is logged in and in use
simpleExplanation: Full-disk encryption protects data best when the device is off or locked. Once the user is logged in and the drive is unlocked, the system can read the data, so malware or an attacker with user access has a better chance of reaching it.
whyCorrect: Full-disk encryption offers least protection after the user logs in and the disk is unlocked for use.

whyIncorrect.A: This option describes During the system boot process. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to When the system is logged in and in use. whyIncorrect.D: This option describes When the system is being shut down. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to When the system is logged in and in use. When off, booting, or shutting down, full-disk encryption still protects locked data better; once logged in, the drive is unlocked and data is accessible to the running system.

question: Before Tony stores a password hash, he appends a string of characters that is unique to each password generated using an algorithm he created. What technique is Tony using to help protect his password hashes?
explanation: Adding a unique value before hashing a password is salting.
simpleExplanation: The question is asking you to recognise the Security+ concept that best fits the situation. The key clue points to Salting. In simple words, Adding a unique value before hashing a password is salting.
whyCorrect: Adding a unique value before hashing a password is salting.

whyIncorrect.A: Tokenization replaces sensitive data with a token that maps back to the original value through a secure lookup system. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to Salting. Tokenization and steganography are obfuscation methods, and key stretching slows hashing; adding a unique string before hashing is salting.
whyIncorrect.B: Steganography hides information inside another file or medium, such as hiding data inside an image. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to Salting. question: After a breach, Jackie removes malicious software from a server that she is responsible for. What control type should she classify this as?
explanation: Removing malware after a breach is corrective because it remediates the problem.
simpleExplanation: The question is asking you to recognise the Security+ concept that best fits the situation. The key clue points to Corrective. In simple words, Removing malware after a breach is corrective because it remediates the problem.
whyCorrect: Removing malware after a breach is corrective because it remediates the problem.

question: What can a root SSL (TLS) certificate do?

options.A: Remove a certificate from a CRL
options.B: Generate a signing key and use it to sign a new certificate

explanation: A root CA uses its root certificate and signing key to sign or validate subordinate certificates in the trust chain.
answerText: Generate a signing key and use it to sign a new certificate
simpleExplanation: The question is asking you to recognise the Security+ concept that best fits the situation. The key clue points to Generate a signing key and use it to sign a new certificate. In simple words, A root CA uses its root certificate and signing key to sign or validate subordinate certificates in the trust chain.
whyCorrect: A root CA uses its root certificate and signing key to sign or validate subordinate certificates in the trust chain.

whyIncorrect.A: A certificate revocation list is used to list certificates that should no longer be trusted. It is not used to cover subdomains. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to Generate a signing key and use it to sign a new certificate. Root certificates do not remove CRL entries, authorize users, or stretch keys; a root CA uses its signing key/certificate chain authority to sign certificates.
whyIncorrect.C: This option describes Authorize new CA users. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to Generate a signing key and use it to sign a new certificate. Root certificates do not remove CRL entries, authorize users, or stretch keys; a root CA uses its signing key/certificate chain authority to sign certificates.
whyIncorrect.D: Key stretching intentionally makes password hashing slower or more computationally expensive to resist brute-force attacks. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to Generate a signing key and use it to sign a new certificate. Root certificates do not remove CRL entries, authorize users, or stretch keys; a root CA uses its signing key/certificate chain authority to sign certificates.

fullExplanation:
The correct answer is B. Generate a signing key and use it to sign a new certificate.

A root CA uses its root certificate and signing key to sign or validate subordinate certificates in the trust chain.

Why the other options are incorrect:
A. Remove a certificate from a CRL: A certificate revocation list is used to list certificates that should no longer be trusted. It is not used to cover subdomains. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to Generate a signing key and use it to sign a new certificate. Root certificates do not remove CRL entries, authorize users, or stretch keys; a root CA uses its signing key/certificate chain authority to sign certificates.
C. Authorize new CA users: This option describes Authorize new CA users. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to Generate a signing key and use it to sign a new certificate. Root certificates do not remove CRL entries, authorize users, or stretch keys; a root CA uses its signing key/certificate chain authority to sign certificates.
D. Allow key stretching: Key stretching intentionally makes password hashing slower or more computationally expensive to resist brute-force attacks. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to Generate a signing key and use it to sign a new certificate. Root certificates do not remove CRL entries, authorize users, or stretch keys; a root CA uses its signing key/certificate chain authority to sign certificates.

question: Christina wants to authenticate individuals as part of her AAA implementation. What will she need to do to authenticate users?

options.A: Match users to roles and ensure that rights are assigned.
options.B: Conduct biometric enrollments for every user.
options.C: Use identity proofing for each user she creates.
options.D: Ensure that users provide an identity and one or more authentication factors.

explanation: Authentication requires the user to present an identity and one or more authentication factors.
answerText: Ensure that users provide an identity and one or more authentication factors.
simpleExplanation: The question is asking you to recognise the Security+ concept that best fits the situation. The key clue points to Ensure that users provide an identity and one or more authentication factors.. In simple words, Authentication requires the user to present an identity and one or more authentication factors.
whyCorrect: Authentication requires the user to present an identity and one or more authentication factors.

whyIncorrect.A: This option describes Match users to roles and ensure that rights are assigned. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to Ensure that users provide an identity and one or more authentication factors.. Roles/rights are authorization, biometrics are only one possible factor, and identity proofing supports enrolment; authentication requires identity plus factors.
whyIncorrect.B: This option describes Conduct biometric enrollments for every user. It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to Ensure that users provide an identity and one or more authentication factors.. Roles/rights are authorization, biometrics are only one possible factor, and identity proofing supports enrolment; authentication requires identity plus factors.

It is not the best answer here because the scenario is specifically pointing to Ensure that users provide an identity and one or more authentication factors.. Roles/rights are authorization, biometrics are only one possible factor, and identity proofing supports enrolment; authentication requires identity plus factors.
